## Funding for CIA fronts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marshall_Plan&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Funding for CIA fronts")]

The [Central Intelligence Agency](/wiki/Central_Intelligence_Agency "Central Intelligence Agency") received 5% of the Marshall Plan funds (about $685 million spread over six years), which it used to finance secret operations abroad. Through the [Office of Policy Coordination](/wiki/Office_of_Policy_Coordination "Office of Policy Coordination") money was directed toward support for labor unions, newspapers, student groups, artists and intellectuals, who were countering the anti-American counterparts subsidized by the communists. The largest sum went to the [Congress for Cultural Freedom](/wiki/Congress_for_Cultural_Freedom "Congress for Cultural Freedom"). There were no agents working among the Soviets or their satellite states.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeiner2007[httpsarchiveorgdetailslegacyofasheshis00weinpage32_32,_40–41]-128) The founding conference of the Congress for Cultural Freedom was held in Berlin in June 1950. Among the leading intellectuals from the US and Western Europe were writers, philosophers, critics and historians: [Raymond Aron](/wiki/Raymond_Aron "Raymond Aron"), [Alfred Ayer](/wiki/Alfred_Ayer "Alfred Ayer"), [Franz Borkenau](/wiki/Franz_Borkenau "Franz Borkenau"), [Irving Brown](/wiki/Irving_Brown "Irving Brown"), [James Burnham](/wiki/James_Burnham "James Burnham"), [Benedetto Croce](/wiki/Benedetto_Croce "Benedetto Croce"), [John Dewey](/wiki/John_Dewey "John Dewey"), [Sidney Hook](/wiki/Sidney_Hook "Sidney Hook"), [Karl Jaspers](/wiki/Karl_Jaspers "Karl Jaspers"), [Arthur Koestler](/wiki/Arthur_Koestler "Arthur Koestler"), [Melvin J. Lasky](/wiki/Melvin_J._Lasky "Melvin J. Lasky"), [Richard Löwenthal](/wiki/Richard_L%C3%B6wenthal "Richard Löwenthal"), [Ernst Reuter](/wiki/Ernst_Reuter "Ernst Reuter"), [Bertrand Russell](/wiki/Bertrand_Russell "Bertrand Russell"), [Arthur M. Schlesinger Jr.](/wiki/Arthur_M._Schlesinger_Jr. "Arthur M. Schlesinger Jr."), [Ignazio Silone](/wiki/Ignazio_Silone "Ignazio Silone"), [Hugh Trevor-Roper](/wiki/Hugh_Trevor-Roper "Hugh Trevor-Roper"), and [Tennessee Williams](/wiki/Tennessee_Williams "Tennessee Williams"). There were conservatives among the participants, but non-communist (or former communist) leftists were more numerous.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJeleński1970Saunders1999-129)

## Effects and legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marshall_Plan&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Effects and legacy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Marshall_Plan_poster.JPG/250px-Marshall_Plan_poster.JPG)](/wiki/File:Marshall_Plan_poster.JPG)

One of the numerous posters created to promote the Marshall Plan in Europe. Note the pivotal position of the American flag. The blue and white flag between those of Germany and Italy is a version of the [Trieste](/wiki/Free_Territory_of_Trieste "Free Territory of Trieste") flag with the UN blue rather than the traditional red.

The Marshall Plan was originally scheduled to end in 1953. Any effort to extend it was halted by the growing cost of the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War") and rearmament. American Republicans hostile to the plan had also gained seats in the [1950 Congressional elections](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives_elections,_1950 "United States House of Representatives elections, 1950"), and conservative opposition to the plan was revived. Thus the plan ended in 1951, though various other forms of American aid to Europe continued afterward.

The years 1948 to 1952 saw the fastest period of growth in European history. Industrial production increased by 35%. Agricultural production substantially surpassed pre-war levels.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrogin2001118-81) The poverty and starvation of the immediate postwar years disappeared, and Western Europe embarked upon an unprecedented two decades of growth that saw standards of living increase dramatically. Additionally, the long-term effect of economic integration raised European income levels substantially, by nearly 20 percent by the mid-1970s.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBadinger200550–78-130) There is some debate among historians over how much this should be credited to the Marshall Plan. Most reject the idea that it alone miraculously revived Europe, as evidence shows that a general recovery was already underway. Most believe that the Marshall Plan sped this recovery, but did not initiate it. Many argue that the structural adjustments that it forced were of great importance. Economic historians [J. Bradford DeLong](/wiki/J._Bradford_DeLong "J. Bradford DeLong") and [Barry Eichengreen](/wiki/Barry_Eichengreen "Barry Eichengreen") call it "history's most successful structural adjustment program."[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeLongEichengreen1993189–230-131) One effect of the plan was that it subtly "Americanized" European countries, especially Austria, through new exposure to American popular culture, including the growth in influence of Hollywood movies and rock n' roll.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBischofPelinkaStiefel2000174–75-132)

The political effects of the Marshall Plan may have been just as important as the economic ones. Marshall Plan aid allowed the nations of Western Europe to relax austerity measures and rationing, reducing discontent and bringing political stability. The communist influence on Western Europe was greatly reduced, and throughout the region, communist parties faded in popularity in the years after the Marshall Plan. The trade relations fostered by the Marshall Plan helped forge the North Atlantic alliance that would persist throughout the Cold War in the form of NATO. At the same time, the nonparticipation of the states of the Eastern Bloc was one of the first clear signs that the continent was now divided.

The Marshall Plan also played an important role in European integration. Both the Americans and many of the European leaders felt that European integration was necessary to secure the peace and prosperity of Europe, and thus used Marshall Plan guidelines to foster integration. In some ways, this effort failed, as the [OEEC](/wiki/OEEC "OEEC") never grew to be more than an agent of economic cooperation. Rather, it was the separate [European Coal and Steel Community](/wiki/European_Coal_and_Steel_Community "European Coal and Steel Community"), which did not include Britain, that would eventually grow into the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"). However, the OEEC served as both a testing and training ground for the structures that would later be used by the [European Economic Community](/wiki/European_Economic_Community "European Economic Community"). The Marshall Plan, linked into the [Bretton Woods system](/wiki/Bretton_Woods_system "Bretton Woods system"), also mandated free trade throughout the region.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Netherlands_10_gulden_Marshall_Plan.jpg/250px-Netherlands_10_gulden_Marshall_Plan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Netherlands_10_gulden_Marshall_Plan.jpg)

Dutch [10 guilder](/wiki/Dutch_guilder "Dutch guilder") coin commemorating the 50th anniversary of the Marshall Plan, 1997

While some historians today feel some of the praise for the Marshall Plan is exaggerated, it is still viewed favorably and many thus feel that a similar project would help other areas of the world. The events of 1947 are thus part and parcel of the larger story of the Cold War. These events played a major role, but it would be a mistake to exaggerate their significance. The bloc system in Europe took years to develop, and the Marshall Plan was just one part of the story.[[125]](#cite_note-133) After the fall of communism, several proposed a "Marshall Plan for Eastern Europe" that would help revive that region. Others have proposed a Marshall Plan for Africa to help that continent, and US [Vice President Al Gore](/wiki/Al_Gore "Al Gore") suggested a [Global Marshall Plan](/wiki/Global_Marshall_Plan "Global Marshall Plan").[[E]](#cite_note-135) "Marshall Plan" has become a metaphor for any very large-scale government program that is designed to solve a specific social problem. It is usually used when calling for federal spending to correct a perceived failure of the private sector.

## Repayment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marshall_Plan&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Repayment")]

The [Organisation for European Economic Co-operation](/wiki/Organisation_for_Economic_Co-operation_and_Development#Organisation_for_European_Economic_Co-operation "Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development") (OEEC) took the leading role in allocating funds, and the OEEC arranged for the transfer of the goods. The American supplier was paid in dollars, which were credited against the appropriate European Recovery Program funds. The European recipient, however, was not given the goods as a gift but had to pay for them (usually on credit) in local currency. These payments were kept by the European government involved in a special **counterpart fund.** This counterpart money, in turn, could be used by the government for further investment projects. Five percent of the counterpart money was paid to the US to cover the administrative costs of the ERP.[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrice1955106-136) In addition to ERP grants, the [Export-Import Bank](/wiki/Export-Import_Bank_of_the_United_States "Export-Import Bank of the United States") (an agency of the US government) at the same time made long-term loans at low interest rates to finance major purchases in the US, all of which were repaid.

In the case of Germany, there also were 16 billion marks of debts from the 1920s which had defaulted in the 1930s, but which Germany decided to repay to restore its reputation. This money was owed to government and private banks in the US, France, and Britain. Another 16 billion marks represented postwar loans by the US. Under the [London Debts Agreement](/wiki/Agreement_on_German_External_Debts "Agreement on German External Debts") of 1953, the repayable amount was reduced by 50% to about 15 billion marks and stretched out over 30 years, and compared to the fast-growing German economy were of minor impact.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuinnane200517,_20–21,_27–28,_30-137)

## Areas excluded

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marshall_Plan&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Areas excluded")]

Large parts of the world devastated by World War II did not benefit from the Marshall Plan. The only major Western European nation excluded was Spain, [whose regime](/wiki/Francoist_Spain "Francoist Spain") under [Francisco Franco](/wiki/Francisco_Franco "Francisco Franco") was highly unpopular in Washington. With the escalation of the Cold War, the United States reconsidered its position and in 1951 embraced Spain as an ally since it was encouraged by Franco's aggressive [anti-communist](/wiki/Anti-communist "Anti-communist") policies. Over the next decade, a considerable amount of American aid would go to Spain but less than its neighbors had received under the Marshall Plan.[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECraftsToniolo1996363-138)

The Soviet Union had been as badly affected as any other part of the world by the war. The Soviets imposed large [reparations](/wiki/War_reparations "War reparations") payments on the Axis allies that were in its sphere of influence. [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria"), [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland"), [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary"), [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania"), and especially [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany") were forced to pay vast sums and ship large amounts of supplies to the Soviet Union. Those reparation payments meant the Soviet Union itself received about the same as 16 European countries received in total from Marshall Plan aid.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZwass198916-139)

In accordance with the agreements with the Soviet Union, shipment of dismantled German industrial installations from the west began on March 31, 1946. Under the terms of the agreement, the Soviet Union would in return ship raw materials such as food and timber to the western zones. In view of the Soviet failure to do so, the western zones halted the shipments east, ostensibly on a temporary basis, although they were never resumed. It was later shown that the main reason for halting shipments east was not the behavior of the Soviet Union but rather the recalcitrant behavior of France.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGimbel1975-140) Examples of material received by the Soviets were equipment from the [Kugel-Fischer](/w/index.php?title=Kugel-Fischer&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kugel-Fischer (page does not exist)") ballbearing plant at [Schweinfurt](/wiki/Schweinfurt "Schweinfurt"), the [Daimler-Benz](/wiki/Daimler-Benz "Daimler-Benz") underground aircraft-engine plant at [Obrigheim](/wiki/Obrigheim "Obrigheim"), the [Deschimag](/wiki/Deschimag "Deschimag") shipyards at [Bremen-Weser](/w/index.php?title=Bremen-Weser&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bremen-Weser (page does not exist)"), and the [Gendorf powerplant](/w/index.php?title=Gendorf_powerplant&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gendorf powerplant (page does not exist)").[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEU._S._Analysis_of_Soviet_Note1957Potsdam_Reparations_Begin1946-141)

The Soviets established [COMECON](/wiki/COMECON "COMECON") as a riposte to the Marshall Plan to deliver aid for Eastern Bloc countries, but that was complicated by the Soviet efforts to manage their own recovery from the war. The members of Comecon looked to the Soviet Union for oil and in turn provided machinery, equipment, agricultural goods, industrial goods, and consumer goods to the Soviet Union. Economic recovery in the East was much slower than in the West, resulting in the formation of the [shortage economies](/wiki/Shortage_economy "Shortage economy") and a gap in wealth between East and West. Finland, which the Soviets forbade from joining the Marshall Plan and was required to give large reparations to the Soviets, saw its economy recover to pre-war levels in 1947.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] France, which received billions of dollars through the Marshall Plan, similarly saw its average income per person return to almost pre-war level by 1949.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDe_LongEichengreen1993202-142) By mid-1948 industrial production in Poland, Hungary, [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria "Bulgaria"), and [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia") had recovered to a level somewhat above pre-war level.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarriner1949157–67-143)

### Aid to Asia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marshall_Plan&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Aid to Asia")]

From the end of the war to the end of 1953, the US provided grants and credits amounting to $5.9 billion to Asian countries, especially the Republic of China (Taiwan) ($1.051 billion), India ($255 million), Indonesia ($215 million), Japan ($2.444 billion), South Korea ($894 million), Pakistan ($98 million) and the Philippines ($803 million). In addition, another $282 million went to Israel and $196 million to the rest of the Middle East.[[F]](#cite_note-census-144) While much of this aid was given during Marshall's tenure as Secretary of State, it was separate from the Marshall Plan itself.[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrice1955179–219-145)

### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marshall_Plan&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Canada")]

Canada's infrastructure was damaged little by the war, as most of the war was fought in Europe, Asia, and the Pacific. In 1948, the US allowed ERP aid to be used to buy goods from Canada. In its first two years of operation, ERP funded over one billion dollars' worth of trade with Canada.[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBothwell199858-146)

### World total

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marshall_Plan&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: World total")]

The total of American grants and loans to the world from 1945 to 1953 came to $44.3 billion.[[F]](#cite_note-census-144)

## Opinion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marshall_Plan&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Opinion")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Schild_Bessere_D%C3%BCngung_des_Amerikanischen_Hilfsprogramms.png/250px-Schild_Bessere_D%C3%BCngung_des_Amerikanischen_Hilfsprogramms.png)](/wiki/File:Schild_Bessere_D%C3%BCngung_des_Amerikanischen_Hilfsprogramms.png)

German sign indicating "agriculture counseling supported by the overseas aid program of the U.S.A."

[Bradford DeLong](/wiki/J._Bradford_DeLong "J. Bradford DeLong") and [Barry Eichengreen](/wiki/Barry_Eichengreen "Barry Eichengreen") conclude it was "History's Most Successful Structural Adjustment Program." They state:

> It was not large enough to have significantly accelerated recovery by financing investment, aiding the reconstruction of damaged infrastructure, or easing commodity bottlenecks. We argue, however, that the Marshall Plan did play a major role in setting the stage for post-World War II Western Europe's rapid growth. The conditions attached to Marshall Plan aid pushed European political economy in a direction that left its post World War II "mixed economies" with more "market" and less "controls" in the mix.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeLongEichengreen1993189–230-131)

### Domestic campaign for support

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marshall_Plan&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Domestic campaign for support")]

Prior to passing and enacting the Marshall Plan, President Truman and George Marshall started a domestic overhaul of public opinion from coast to coast. The purpose of this campaign was to sway public opinion in their direction and to inform the common person of what the Marshall Plan was and what the plan would ultimately do. They spent months attempting to convince Americans that their cause was just and that they should embrace the higher taxes that would come in the foreseeable future.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMachado2007-147) The Great Depression left Americans acutely aware of the psychological and political effects of poverty. Though the Marshall Plan is seen as an act of compassion or sympathy for countries struggling after WWII, it is likely fear of slipping into another depression at home caused the United States to invest in diplomacy and pivot away from another economic calamity.[[138]](#cite_note-148)

A copious amount of propaganda ended up being highly effective in swaying public opinion toward supporting the Marshall Plan. During the nationwide campaign for support, "more than a million pieces of pro-Marshall Plan publications-booklets, leaflets, reprints, and fact sheets", were disseminated.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELukacs1997-149) Truman's and Marshall's efforts proved to be effective. A Gallup Poll taken between the months of July and December 1947 shows the percentage of Americans unaware of the Marshall Plan fell from 51% to 36% nationwide.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMachado2007-147) By the time the Marshall Plan was ready to be implemented, there was a general consensus throughout the American public that this was the right policy for both America and the countries who would be receiving aid.

### Change in American ideology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marshall_Plan&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Change in American ideology")]

During the period leading up to World War II, Americans were highly isolationist, and many called The Marshall Plan a "milestone" for American ideology.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELukacs1997-149) By looking at polling data over time from pre-World War II to post-World War II, one would find that there was a change in public opinion in regards to ideology. Americans swapped their isolationist ideals for a much more global internationalist ideology after World War II.

### Polling data

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marshall_Plan&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Polling data")]

In a [National Opinion Research Center](/wiki/NORC_at_the_University_of_Chicago "NORC at the University of Chicago") (NORC) poll taken in April 1945, a cross-section of Americans were asked, "If our government keeps on sending lendlease materials, which we may not get paid for, to friendly countries for about three years after the war, do you think this will mean more jobs or fewer jobs for most Americans, or won't it make any difference?" 57% said more jobs, 18% no-change in jobs; 10% said fewer.[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECantrilStrunk1951414-150)

Before proposing anything to Congress in 1947, the Truman administration made an elaborate effort to organize public opinion in favor of the Marshall Plan spending, reaching out to numerous national organizations representing business, labor, farmers, women, and other interest groups. Political scientist Ralph Levering points out that:

> Mounting large public relations campaigns and supporting private groups such as the Citizens [Committee for the Marshall Plan](/wiki/Committee_for_the_Marshall_Plan "Committee for the Marshall Plan"), the administration carefully built public and bipartisan Congressional support before bringing these measures to a vote.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELevering197898–99-151)

Public opinion polls in 1947 consistently showed strong support for the Marshall plan among Americans. Furthermore, Gallup polls in England, France, and Italy showed favorable majorities over 60%.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGallup1972691,_708–09,_722-152)

## Criticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marshall_Plan&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Criticism")]

### Laissez-faire criticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marshall_Plan&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Laissez-faire criticism")]

[Laissez-faire](/wiki/Laissez-faire "Laissez-faire") criticism of the Marshall Plan came from a number of economists. [Wilhelm Röpke](/wiki/Wilhelm_R%C3%B6pke "Wilhelm Röpke"), who influenced [German Minister for Economy](/wiki/Federal_Minister_of_Economics_and_Technology_(Germany) "Federal Minister of Economics and Technology (Germany)") [Ludwig Erhard](/wiki/Ludwig_Erhard "Ludwig Erhard") in his [economic recovery program](/wiki/Wirtschaftswunder "Wirtschaftswunder"), believed recovery would be found in eliminating [central planning](/wiki/Central_planning "Central planning") and restoring a market economy in Europe, especially in those countries which had adopted more [fascist](/wiki/Fascism "Fascism") and [corporatist](/wiki/Corporatism "Corporatism") economic policies. Röpke criticized the Marshall Plan for forestalling the transition to the free market by subsidizing the current, failing systems. Erhard put Röpke's theory into practice and would later credit Röpke's influence for West Germany's preeminent success.[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEErhard22-153)[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZmirak2001-154)

[Henry Hazlitt](/wiki/Henry_Hazlitt "Henry Hazlitt") criticized the Marshall Plan in his 1947 book *Will Dollars Save the World?*, arguing that economic recovery comes through savings, [capital accumulation](/wiki/Capital_accumulation "Capital accumulation"), and private enterprise, and not through large cash subsidies. [Austrian School](/wiki/Austrian_School "Austrian School") economist [Ludwig von Mises](/wiki/Ludwig_von_Mises "Ludwig von Mises") criticized the Marshall Plan in 1951, believing that "the American subsidies make it possible for [Europe's] governments to conceal partially the disastrous effects of the various socialist measures they have adopted".[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVon_Mises2006-155)

### Modern criticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marshall_Plan&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Modern criticism")]

The Marshall Plan's role in the rapid recovery of Western Europe has been debated. Most reject the idea that it alone miraculously revived Europe since the evidence shows that a general recovery was already underway. The Marshall Plan grants were provided at a rate that was not much higher in terms of flow than the previous [UNRRA](/wiki/UNRRA "UNRRA") aid and represented less than 3% of the combined [national income](/wiki/National_income "National income") of the recipient countries between 1948 and 1951,[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeLongEichengreen1993189–230-131) which would mean an increase in [GDP](/wiki/GDP "GDP") growth of only 0.3%.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrafts20116-10) In addition, there is no correlation between the amount of aid received and the speed of recovery: both [France](/wiki/France "France") and the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") received more aid, but [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany") recovered significantly faster.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrafts20116-10)

Criticism of the Marshall Plan became prominent among historians of the [revisionist](/wiki/Historical_revisionism "Historical revisionism") school, such as [Walter LaFeber](/wiki/Walter_LaFeber "Walter LaFeber"), during the 1960s and 1970s. They argued that the plan was American [economic imperialism](/wiki/Economic_imperialism "Economic imperialism") and that it was an attempt to gain control over Western Europe just as the Soviets controlled Eastern Europe economically through the [Comecon](/wiki/Comecon "Comecon"). In a review of West Germany's economy from 1945 to 1951, German analyst [Werner Abelshauser](/wiki/Werner_Abelshauser "Werner Abelshauser") concluded that "foreign aid was not crucial in starting the recovery or in keeping it going". The economic recoveries of France, Italy, and Belgium, Cowen argues, began a few months before the flow of US money. Belgium, the country that relied earliest and most heavily on free-market economic policies after its liberation in 1944, experienced swift recovery and avoided the severe housing and food shortages seen in the rest of continental Europe.[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarshall_Plan_for_Iraq?2003-156)

Former US Chairman of the Federal Reserve Bank [Alan Greenspan](/wiki/Alan_Greenspan "Alan Greenspan") gives most credit to German Chancellor [Ludwig Erhard](/wiki/Ludwig_Erhard "Ludwig Erhard") for Europe's economic recovery. Greenspan writes in his memoir *[The Age of Turbulence](/wiki/The_Age_of_Turbulence "The Age of Turbulence")* that Erhard's economic policies were the most important aspect of postwar Western European recovery, even outweighing the contributions of the Marshall Plan. He states that it was Erhard's reductions in economic regulations that permitted Germany's miraculous recovery, and that these policies also contributed to the recoveries of many other European countries. Its recovery is attributed to traditional economic stimuli, such as increases in investment, fueled by a high savings rate and low taxes. Japan saw a large infusion of US investment during the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War").[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEForsberg200084-157)

The Marshall Plan has been recently reinterpreted as a public policy approach to complex and multi-causal problems in search of building integrated solutions with multilevel governance.[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELassance20211–59-158)

### In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marshall_Plan&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

The 1953 Spanish comedy film *[Welcome Mr. Marshall!](/wiki/Welcome_Mr._Marshall! "Welcome Mr. Marshall!")* depicts Castilian villagers preparing for a visit of the American authorities and hoping they will fulfill their desires.
When the American motorcade arrives, it speeds through the village and the villagers themselves have to pay for the preparations.
Underneath the surface, the film is an anti-[Francoist](/wiki/Francoist_Spain "Francoist Spain") satire despite receiving approval because the censorship board understood it to be an anti-American satire.[[149]](#cite_note-159)
